title: Laying ceramic floor tiles
subtitle: Find the centre, bed in adhesive, grout when set
excerpt: How to tile a floor in ceramic tiles: finding the room centre, establishing setting-out lines, spreading tile adhesive, laying tiles with consistent spacing, cutting border tiles, and grouting to a clean finish.
sourceNotes: Standard UK tile-laying practice. British Standards BS 5385 Part 3 (floor tiling). City & Guilds Tiling Level 2 syllabus. Manufacturers' guidelines from Mapei and BAL adhesive products.

content.text: Floor tiling is a setting-out exercise first and a laying exercise second. The tiles in the centre of the room determine the entire pattern; the cut tiles at the border follow from where the centre tiles land. Get the centre lines wrong and the border cuts are uneven. Get them right and the floor lays itself.
What you'll need
Ceramic floor tiles and matching grout.
Measure the floor area and add 10 percent for cuts and breakage. Buy enough to finish the job in one batch; tile colours vary between batches.
Tile adhesive.
Use a flexible floor-tile adhesive rated for the substrate. Ready-mixed or powder-mix. A notched spreader (notch size to match tile size) is included with most bags or sold separately.
Tile spacers.
Plastic cross-shaped spacers maintain consistent grout lines. 2 to 3 mm for floor tiles, 3 to 5 mm for larger format tiles.
Tile cutter or wet-saw.
A manual score-and-snap cutter handles straight cuts in most ceramic tiles up to 400 × 400 mm. A wet tile saw cuts curves and difficult cuts.
Notched trowel (6 mm V-notch for tiles up to 300 × 300 mm).
For spreading the adhesive in ridges so the tile can bed fully flat.
Grout float.
A rubber-faced float for pressing grout into the joints. A damp sponge and clean water for cleaning up.
Subfloor preparation
Step 1, check and prepare the subfloor.

The subfloor must be solid, level to within 3 mm over 1800 mm, and free of movement. Ceramic tiles will crack if the subfloor flexes. On a timber floor, add a layer of 12 mm exterior-grade ply screwed at 150 mm centres to stiffen the deck. On concrete, fill depressions with self-levelling compound and allow to cure.
Setting out
Step 2, find the room centre.

Find the midpoint of each pair of opposite walls and snap chalk lines between them. The intersection is the room centre. Lay a dry row of tiles from this point to each wall (without adhesive) to check where the border cut will fall. Adjust the centre point if the border cut at any wall would be less than half a tile; it is better to move the centre than to end with slivers at the door.
Step 3, establish setting-out lines.

Snap or draw two chalk lines at 90 degrees to each other through the adjusted centre point. Check the lines are truly square with each other using the 3-4-5 method: measure 900 mm along one line and 1200 mm along the other; the diagonal between these two points should be exactly 1500 mm if the angle is true.
Laying tiles
Step 4, spread the adhesive.

Mix the adhesive to a firm, lump-free paste. Spread a working area of approximately 1 square metre with the notched trowel, combing the ridges at a consistent angle. Do not spread more than you can tile in 10 to 15 minutes; the adhesive skins over and loses its grip.
Step 5, lay the first tiles.

Press the first tile into the adhesive with a slight twisting action to collapse the ridges, and tap it level with a rubber mallet. Place spacers at all four corners. Lay the tiles outward from the centre in a pyramid pattern, checking level frequently with the spirit level. Do not slide tiles once placed; this pushes adhesive up into the grout lines.
Step 6, cut and fit the border tiles.

Allow the field tiles to set for 24 hours before cutting border tiles. To mark a border tile: place a full tile face-down on the last full tile in the row, butt a second tile against the wall, and mark the bottom tile along the edge of the top tile. Cut along the mark and fit with the cut edge to the wall. The uncut edge butts the field.
Grouting
Step 7, grout the joints.

Allow the adhesive to cure fully (24 hours minimum; check the product data) before grouting. Remove the spacers. Mix the grout to a smooth paste and spread it diagonally across the tiles with the grout float, pressing it firmly into the joints. Remove the excess from the tile face with the float edge held at 45 degrees. Work in 1-square-metre sections.
Step 8, clean and polish.

After 15 to 20 minutes, wipe the tile surface with a barely damp sponge, rinsing the sponge frequently. Do not wash water into the grout lines at this stage. Once the grout has hardened, polish the tile surface with a dry cloth to remove the grout haze.
Common mistakes
Tiles cracking after a few months.

The subfloor was not rigid enough. Check adjacent tiles for hollow spots by tapping with a coin: a dull sound indicates the tile is not bonded to the adhesive below. This floor needs to be lifted and re-laid on a stiffer substrate.
Uneven grout lines.
Spacers were removed before the adhesive set, or tiles were pressed unevenly. Even grout lines require consistent spacers throughout; do not skip them on any tile.
